stem: You are the obstetric registrar reviewing a 29-year-old G1P0 woman at 32+1 weeks gestation who has presented with a gush of clear fluid 2 hours ago and ongoing damp pad. She has no contractions and no bleeding. The fetal movements are normal. The pregnancy has been uncomplicated. Speculum examination confirms pooling of fluid in the vagina and a closed cervix.

data_table.Examination: Speculum: pooling of clear amniotic fluid, no bleeding, closed cervix; abdomen soft, no contractions
data_table.Investigations: CTG reassuring; FBC normal; CRP 4; group B Strep swab taken; ultrasound: AFI 6 cm, fetal weight 1850 g

lead_in: What is the single most appropriate management bundle?

options.text: Admit; corticosteroids and oral erythromycin 250 mg 6-hourly for 10 days; expectant management to 34 weeks if stable
options.rationale: PPROM between 24 and 34 weeks is managed expectantly with corticosteroids, latency antibiotics (erythromycin per ORACLE I), inpatient observation and delivery at 34 weeks unless infection, abruption or fetal compromise. (Source: RANZCOG: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Discharge home with twice-weekly outpatient review and instructions to return if temperature 38 or above; expectant care
options.rationale: Outpatient management of PPROM is associated with higher rates of cord prolapse and chorioamnionitis going undetected; inpatient monitoring is standard in Australia. (Source: RANZCOG: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Immediate caesarean section after a single corticosteroid dose; transfer to a tertiary neonatal centre for late preterm delivery
options.rationale: Without infection, abruption or non-reassuring CTG, immediate delivery at 32 weeks exposes the neonate to prematurity complications without benefit. (Source: RANZCOG: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Augment with intravenous oxytocin; aim for vaginal birth within 24 hours; corticosteroids only if cervix is unfavourable
options.rationale: Active augmentation at 32 weeks without infection or compromise causes iatrogenic prematurity; expectant management with corticosteroid cover prolongs gestation safely. (Source: RANZCOG: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

options.text: Empirical intravenous ampicillin and gentamicin for chorioamnionitis; defer corticosteroids until temperature trend established
options.rationale: Without fever, fetal tachycardia, raised CRP or uterine tenderness there is no chorioamnionitis; corticosteroids should not be delayed when PPROM is confirmed between 24 and 34 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

explanation.summary: PPROM between 24 and 34 weeks is managed expectantly with admission, corticosteroids (betamethasone 11.4 mg IM, two doses 24 hours apart) for fetal lung maturity, latency antibiotics with erythromycin per the ORACLE I trial, and inpatient surveillance for chorioamnionitis (temperature, fetal heart rate, CRP, white cell count). Magnesium sulfate is given for neuroprotection if delivery is anticipated within 24 hours below 30 weeks. Delivery is planned at 34 weeks unless infection, abruption, cord prolapse or fetal compromise mandates earlier delivery.
key_points: Expectant management 24 to 34 weeks with admission and surveillance
Corticosteroids plus erythromycin latency antibiotics standard
Magnesium for neuroprotection below 30 weeks if delivery imminent
Plan delivery at 34 weeks unless complications
explanation.pearls: Co-amoxiclav is avoided as latency antibiotic in PPROM because of increased necrotising enterocolitis risk; erythromycin remains the agent of choice.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Preterm prelabour rupture of membranes

subtopic_detail: Severe pre-eclampsia magnesium loading and target
stem: You are the obstetric registrar in birth suite reviewing a 28-year-old G2P1 woman at 34 weeks with new severe-range hypertension and frontal headache that has not responded to paracetamol. Her booking BP was 110/68. She has 3+ proteinuria on dipstick. She is otherwise well and the CTG is reassuring. She has no asthma. The blood tests have just returned.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/112 repeated, HR 92, RR 18, SpO2 99% RA, T 36.8
data_table.Examination: Brisk reflexes; no clonus; mild facial oedema; abdomen soft; fundal height 33 cm
data_table.Investigations: Plt 124, ALT 84, urate 0.42, Cr 76, LDH 320, PCR 380 mg/mmol; urgent labetalol bolus given
data_table.Medications: Pregnancy multivitamin; nifedipine PR 20 mg given 20 minutes ago

lead_in: Which magnesium regimen and seizure-prevention rationale is most appropriate?

options.text: Magnesium sulfate 1 g IV bolus over 5 minutes then 0.5 g/hour infusion; recheck reflexes and respiratory rate at 4 hours
options.rationale: The dose is subtherapeutic; standard MAGPIE-derived loading is 4 g IV over 20 minutes then 1 g/hour, which delivers seizure prevention without ongoing escalation. (Source: RANZCOG: Hypertension in pregnancy)

options.text: Magnesium sulfate 4 g IV over 20 minutes then 1 g/hour infusion for 24 hours; monitor reflexes and respiratory rate hourly
options.rationale: Standard Australian regimen for severe pre-eclampsia is magnesium sulfate 4 g IV loading over 20 minutes then 1 g/hour for 24 hours, derived from the MAGPIE trial showing approximately halving of eclampsia risk. (Source: RANZCOG: Hypertension in pregnancy)

options.text: Phenytoin 15 mg/kg IV loading dose over 30 minutes; commence maintenance phenytoin 100 mg every 8 hours for 7 days
options.rationale: Phenytoin is inferior to magnesium for eclampsia prophylaxis based on the Collaborative Eclampsia Trial; magnesium is the standard agent. (Source: RANZCOG: Hypertension in pregnancy)

options.text: Diazepam 10 mg IV bolus and commence midazolam 5 mg/hour infusion; reassess BP control after sedation
options.rationale: Benzodiazepines are not first-line eclampsia prophylaxis and cause maternal and neonatal respiratory depression; magnesium has clear superiority. (Source: RANZCOG: Hypertension in pregnancy)

options.text: Reserve magnesium for after a first seizure; commence intravenous hydralazine and aim for BP below 140/90 in the next hour
options.rationale: Severe pre-eclampsia with severe features requires immediate magnesium prophylaxis without waiting for eclampsia; reactive management increases morbidity. (Source: RANZCOG: Hypertension in pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Magnesium sulfate is the standard agent for both eclampsia prophylaxis in severe pre-eclampsia and treatment of eclamptic seizures. The Australian regimen is 4 g IV loading over 20 minutes followed by 1 g/hour infusion for 24 hours. Monitoring includes hourly respiratory rate, reflexes and urine output; magnesium toxicity is treated with 1 g calcium gluconate IV. BP target during severe pre-eclampsia is below 160/110, achieved with IV labetalol, hydralazine or oral nifedipine; aim is not normotension but reduction of stroke risk.
key_points: Magnesium 4 g IV load then 1 g/hour for 24 hours
Hourly RR, reflexes and urine output monitoring
Calcium gluconate 1 g IV reverses magnesium toxicity
BP target below 160/110, not normotension
explanation.pearls: Magnesium also provides neonatal neuroprotection when delivery is anticipated below 30 weeks gestation, with a 30 percent reduction in cerebral palsy risk.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar called urgently to a birth suite. A 33-year-old G3P3 woman delivered a 4200 g infant 25 minutes ago after a prolonged second stage with vacuum extraction. Estimated blood loss is now 1500 mL and ongoing. She received 10 IU IM oxytocin at delivery and 40 IU oxytocin infusion plus 250 mcg ergometrine IM 10 minutes ago. The uterus is still atonic. There are no obvious tears. She has no pre-existing hypertension and no asthma.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 92/58, HR 124, RR 22, SpO2 98% on 4 L/min, T 36.4
data_table.Examination: Uterus boggy, fundus at umbilicus; placenta delivered intact; vagina and perineum inspected, no major tear
data_table.Investigations: Hb 78, plt 156, fibrinogen 2.0, INR 1.2, blood group and screen, two large bore IV lines in situ
data_table.Medications: Oxytocin 40 IU infusion at 250 mL/h; ergometrine 250 mcg IM given 10 min ago

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next pharmacological and procedural step?

options.text: Repeat ergometrine 250 mcg intramuscular; continue oxytocin infusion and reassess uterine tone in 15 minutes
options.rationale: Maximum ergometrine dose in 24 hours is 1 mg (5 doses of 200 mcg) and the patient is already 250 mcg in; a second-line uterotonic should be added in addition. (Source: RANZCOG: PPH)

options.text: Take to theatre for laparotomy and B-Lynch suture; defer further pharmacological uterotonics pending operative intervention
options.rationale: Surgical intervention is reserved for failure of mechanical and pharmacological measures including intrauterine balloon and prostaglandin; uterine atony often responds to escalation without laparotomy. (Source: RANZCOG: PPH)

options.text: Carboprost 250 mcg intramuscular and intrauterine balloon tamponade; continue oxytocin infusion and activate massive transfusion protocol
options.rationale: Refractory atonic PPH after first-line oxytocin and ergometrine warrants escalation to carboprost (15-methyl PGF2 alpha) plus intrauterine balloon tamponade and activation of massive transfusion in the setting of ongoing significant blood loss. (Source: RANZCOG: PPH)

options.text: Tranexamic acid 4 g intravenous bolus; reassess uterine tone and bleeding in 30 minutes before further escalation
options.rationale: Tranexamic acid 1 g IV is given alongside other measures within 3 hours of PPH onset per the WOMAN trial; 4 g is not standard and TXA alone does not address ongoing atony. (Source: WOMAN Trial)

options.text: Manual uterine compression and bimanual massage only; cease pharmacological therapy and observe for spontaneous tone return
options.rationale: Bimanual compression buys time but stopping pharmacotherapy is unsafe; uterotonic escalation is required for ongoing atonic haemorrhage with haemodynamic compromise. (Source: RANZCOG: PPH)

explanation.summary: PPH management combines pharmacological and mechanical escalation with parallel resuscitation. First line is uterine massage plus oxytocin (IM and IV); second line adds ergometrine (avoid in hypertension) and carboprost (avoid in severe asthma). Tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3 hours of onset reduces mortality. Intrauterine balloon tamponade is the next mechanical step before surgical options. Surgical interventions include B-Lynch, uterine artery ligation, and hysterectomy when medical and tamponade therapy fail.
key_points: Stepped escalation: oxytocin, ergometrine, carboprost, balloon tamponade
TXA 1 g IV within 3 hours of PPH onset
Carboprost contraindicated in severe asthma
Surgical options after failure of medical and tamponade therapy
explanation.pearls: Activate massive transfusion protocol when blood loss exceeds 1500 mL with ongoing bleeding or shock index above 1; aim for FFP and platelets at 1:1:1 ratio.

stem: You are the obstetric registrar in antenatal clinic reviewing a 30-year-old G3P2 woman at 36 weeks who wants to plan a trial of labour after caesarean. Her first caesarean at 38 weeks was for failure to progress, with a classical incision used because of marked adhesions. Her second was an elective lower-segment caesarean. The current pregnancy is uncomplicated and the baby is cephalic with estimated weight 3300 g. The placenta is anterior and fundal. She is keen to avoid another caesarean.

data_table.Examination: Uterus appropriate for dates; cephalic presentation; fetal heart rate 142 bpm; pelvis adequate clinically
data_table.Investigations: Ultrasound: anterior fundal placenta, no praevia, cervix 30 mm long, EFW 3300 g

lead_in: What is the most appropriate counselling and plan?

options.text: Plan vaginal birth after caesarean with continuous CTG; epidural analgesia available; oxytocin augmentation as required
options.rationale: VBAC is contraindicated after a previous classical caesarean incision because of high uterine rupture risk; the lower-segment incision in the second caesarean does not negate the classical scar risk. (Source: RANZCOG: VBAC)

options.text: Plan vaginal birth after caesarean with mechanical cervical ripening; avoid prostaglandins; reassess if spontaneous labour delays
options.rationale: Mechanical ripening with a previous classical incision does not eliminate uterine rupture risk; the classical scar precludes trial of labour. (Source: RANZCOG: VBAC)

options.text: Plan vaginal birth after caesarean only if she goes into spontaneous labour at term; elective caesarean if she has not laboured by 41 weeks
options.rationale: Spontaneous labour does not lower the unacceptable rupture risk of a classical scar; planned elective caesarean is recommended at 36 to 37 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: VBAC)

options.text: Counsel that classical caesarean precludes trial of labour; plan elective repeat caesarean at 36 to 37 weeks
options.rationale: Previous classical caesarean carries 4 to 9 percent uterine rupture risk and is a contraindication to trial of labour; elective caesarean at 36 to 37 weeks is recommended to pre-empt spontaneous labour. (Source: RANZCOG: VBAC)

options.text: Refer for external cephalic version at 37 weeks if breech; otherwise allow spontaneous labour with intrapartum review
options.rationale: Baby is already cephalic and ECV is not relevant; the central issue is the classical scar which contraindicates trial of labour irrespective of presentation. (Source: RANZCOG: VBAC)

explanation.summary: Vaginal birth after caesarean is appropriate for selected women with one previous lower-segment caesarean and no other contraindication, with success rates of approximately 70 percent. Absolute contraindications include previous classical or inverted-T uterine incision, previous uterine rupture, previous myomectomy entering the cavity, and standard contraindications to vaginal birth (placenta praevia, transverse lie). Planned elective caesarean is performed at 36 to 37 weeks for classical incisions to pre-empt spontaneous labour-induced rupture.
key_points: Classical or inverted-T incision = absolute VBAC contraindication
Elective repeat caesarean at 36 to 37 weeks for classical
VBAC after one LSCS has approximately 70 percent success
Continuous CTG and IV access required during TOLAC
explanation.pearls: Uterine rupture rate after one LSCS during trial of labour is approximately 0.5 percent; classical incision rupture rate is up to 10 percent, mostly during spontaneous labour.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top Guideline: Birth After Previous Caesarean

stem: You are the obstetric registrar called to ED to review a 31-year-old G2P1 woman at 33 weeks gestation with painless bright red vaginal bleeding starting 1 hour ago. The 20-week scan reported anterior low-lying placenta and a follow-up scan at 32 weeks confirmed complete placenta praevia. She estimates 250 mL of blood loss and is now pad changing every 20 minutes. The fetus is moving normally. She has no contractions and no abdominal pain.

data_table.Examination: Abdomen soft, non-tender; uterus not contracting; vagina with ongoing fresh bleeding; speculum not performed yet; no vaginal examination done
data_table.Investigations: Hb 102, plt 168, group and screen on the way; CTG shows reassuring fetal heart rate 140 bpm with normal variability

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Vaginal examination to assess cervical dilatation; commence intravenous oxytocin to expedite delivery vaginally
options.rationale: Vaginal examination is contraindicated in known placenta praevia because it can precipitate massive haemorrhage; vaginal birth is not possible with complete praevia. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia and accreta)

options.text: Discharge home after observation if bleeding settles within 2 hours; outpatient ultrasound follow-up in 1 week
options.rationale: Placenta praevia with active bleeding requires admission; discharge risks recurrent or massive haemorrhage outside a hospital setting. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia and accreta)

options.text: Administer external cephalic version to facilitate vaginal birth; corticosteroids and observe for delivery within 24 hours
options.rationale: ECV is contraindicated in placenta praevia and irrelevant to a cephalic-presenting fetus; placenta praevia mandates caesarean birth. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia and accreta)

options.text: Activate massive transfusion and proceed to immediate caesarean section under general anaesthesia regardless of fetal status
options.rationale: With stable maternal and fetal status, immediate caesarean is not required; corticosteroids and observation can prolong gestation safely with appropriate monitoring. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia and accreta)

options.text: Admit; large bore IV access, fluid resuscitation, group and crossmatch 2 units; corticosteroids and anti-D if Rh negative
options.rationale: Stable placenta praevia with APH is admitted, IV access secured, crossmatched blood available, corticosteroids given for fetal lung maturity if 24 to 34 weeks, anti-D if Rh negative, and delivery planned at 36 to 37 weeks unless ongoing haemorrhage. (Source: RANZCOG: Placenta praevia and accreta)

explanation.summary: Antepartum haemorrhage in known placenta praevia mandates admission, large-bore IV access, fluid resuscitation if needed, and group and crossmatch readiness. Vaginal examination is contraindicated. Corticosteroids are given at 24 to 34 weeks for fetal lung maturity. Anti-D is given to Rh-negative women. Continuous monitoring of mother and fetus continues until bleeding settles. Elective caesarean at 36 to 37 weeks is standard, brought forward if haemorrhage recurs or becomes massive.
key_points: No vaginal examination in known placenta praevia
Admit, IV access, crossmatch, monitor
Corticosteroids 24 to 34 weeks for fetal lung maturity
Elective caesarean planned at 36 to 37 weeks
explanation.pearls: Placenta praevia plus prior caesarean increases placenta accreta spectrum risk; tertiary centre delivery with cell-salvage and interventional radiology backup is recommended.

stem: You are the community mental health registrar reviewing a 24-year-old woman with a 4-year history of intense unstable relationships, recurrent self-harm by superficial cutting, chronic feelings of emptiness, and impulsive spending. She has had 3 brief inpatient admissions for safety after threats of self-harm. She is not currently suicidal. There is no active substance use disorder, no eating disorder, and her PHQ-9 today is 7 (mild). She wants to feel more stable in herself.

data_table.Examination: Healed superficial linear scars on forearms; reactive affect; no current SI; no thought disorder; no psychotic features
data_table.Investigations: PHQ-9 7, GAD-7 9, ASRS negative, AUDIT 3; FBC, U&E, TSH normal

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate evidence-based first-line intervention?

options.text: Refer for dialectical behaviour therapy; arrange care coordination with crisis plan and skills coaching access
options.rationale: Structured psychological therapy is first-line in BPD; DBT has the strongest evidence base, with mentalisation-based therapy and schema-focused therapy as alternatives. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Personality Disorders)

options.text: Commence olanzapine 5 mg nocte and arrange psychiatry review in 4 weeks for response and side effects
options.rationale: Antipsychotics are not first-line and are reserved for short-term symptom-targeted use (transient psychotic phenomena, severe anger); psychotherapy comes first. (Source: NICE BPD Guideline)

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily; review at 4 weeks for response and adverse effects with structured measurement
options.rationale: Antidepressants are not first-line for BPD without comorbid major depression; symptom-targeted prescribing in BPD has limited evidence and may delay psychotherapy access. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Personality Disorders)

options.text: Arrange weekly individual supportive psychotherapy alone; review every 12 weeks with non-structured care coordination
options.rationale: Supportive therapy alone is inferior to structured therapies designed for BPD such as DBT, MBT or schema-focused therapy. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Personality Disorders)

options.text: Admit for short-term inpatient psychiatric care; commence quetiapine 50 mg nocte during stabilisation phase
options.rationale: Prolonged inpatient care is not recommended in BPD and can worsen outcomes; community-based structured therapy is the standard. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Personality Disorders)

explanation.summary: Borderline personality disorder is treated primarily with structured evidence-based psychological therapies. Dialectical behaviour therapy (DBT) has the strongest evidence base, with mentalisation-based therapy and schema-focused therapy as effective alternatives. Pharmacotherapy is symptom-targeted, short-term, and reserved for crisis or comorbidity; no medication is licensed for BPD itself. Prolonged inpatient care is avoided. Crisis plans, care coordination and skills coaching access are key adjuncts.
key_points: DBT is the most evidence-based first-line therapy
MBT and schema-focused therapy are effective alternatives
No medication is first-line for BPD itself
Avoid prolonged inpatient care
explanation.pearls: DBT consists of weekly individual therapy plus group skills training across mindfulness, distress tolerance, emotion regulation and interpersonal effectiveness, typically over 12 months.

stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 32-year-old man who was a passenger in a fatal car accident 6 months ago. He has flashbacks 3 times daily, sleep disturbance with nightmares 4 nights per week, avoids driving on motorways, and is hypervigilant. He drinks 2 standard drinks per week, no other substance use. He has no past psychiatric history. He is keen to engage in treatment and prefers psychological therapy over medication. PHQ-9 today is 14 (moderate).

data_table.Examination: Anxious but cooperative; intrusive thoughts of the accident on direct questioning; intact thought form; no psychosis; no SI
data_table.Investigations: PCL-5 score 52; PHQ-9 14; AUDIT 3; thyroid and CMP normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily titrated to 100 mg at 4 weeks; review response at 6 weeks
options.rationale: SSRIs are effective but psychological therapy is preferred first-line where available and acceptable; this patient prefers therapy and meets criteria for it. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for trauma-focused cognitive behavioural therapy; review response after 8 to 12 sessions
options.rationale: Trauma-focused CBT (including prolonged exposure and cognitive processing therapy) and EMDR are first-line treatments for PTSD with the strongest evidence; psychological therapy is preferred over pharmacotherapy where accessible. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Commence prazosin 1 mg nocte titrated for nightmares; review and add sertraline at 4 weeks if no response
options.rationale: Prazosin can target nightmares but is adjunctive, not first-line for PTSD as a whole; trauma-focused therapy addresses the broader syndrome. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Recommend supportive counselling and watchful waiting for 3 months before commencing any active intervention
options.rationale: Active PTSD at 6 months post-trauma warrants treatment now; watchful waiting is appropriate only in the first 4 weeks post-trauma. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Commence quetiapine 50 mg nocte for sleep and anxiety; review response at 4 weeks for symptom control
options.rationale: Quetiapine is not first-line for PTSD and exposes the patient to metabolic and movement side effects without addressing trauma memory processing. (Source: Phoenix Australia PTSD Guidelines)

explanation.summary: First-line treatment for adult PTSD is trauma-focused psychological therapy, with prolonged exposure, cognitive processing therapy or EMDR carrying the strongest evidence. Where therapy is unavailable, unacceptable to the patient, or insufficient, SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) or venlafaxine are first-line pharmacotherapies. Watchful waiting is for the first 4 weeks post-trauma only. Prazosin and atypical antipsychotics are adjunctive options for specific symptoms.
key_points: Trauma-focused CBT and EMDR are first line
SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) or venlafaxine when psychotherapy unavailable
Watchful waiting only in the first 4 weeks post-trauma
Avoid benzodiazepines for PTSD
explanation.pearls: Prolonged exposure and cognitive processing therapy show effect sizes around 1.0; benefit usually emerges within 8 to 12 weekly sessions.

subtopic_detail: Adult ASD recognition in women with masking
stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 28-year-old woman with a 12-year history of recurrent burnout from work and social demands. She describes feeling like she is acting in social situations and reports intense sensory sensitivity to fluorescent lights and clothing tags. She has narrow restricted interests in 19th century botany. She has a history of anorexia nervosa and a previous diagnosis of social anxiety. Her cousin has autism. She has come because she suspects she might be autistic.

data_table.Examination: Eye contact intermittent; affect appropriate; no thought disorder; no psychotic features; settled mood
data_table.Investigations: AQ-50 score 38, RAADS-R score 168, no hearing or vision deficit; previous PHQ-9 8

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Reassure that adult diagnosis of autism is uncommon in women; address presenting anxiety with CBT and lifestyle measures
options.rationale: Adult diagnosis of autism in women is increasingly recognised due to masking and previous misdiagnosis; this presentation warrants formal assessment. (Source: RANZCP: Autism Spectrum Disorder in Adults)
source_refs: RANZCP: Autism Spectrum Disorder in Adults

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily for social anxiety; review in 4 weeks for response and tolerability
options.rationale: Antidepressants address comorbid anxiety but do not constitute assessment for autism, which would change formulation and support needs. (Source: RANZCP: ASD in Adults)
source_refs: RANZCP: Autism Spectrum Disorder in Adults

options.text: Refer for formal multidisciplinary autism assessment using ADOS-2 and developmental history with collateral
options.rationale: Suggestive screening scores plus presenting features warrant formal multidisciplinary assessment using gold-standard tools (ADOS-2, ADI-R or developmental history with collateral); adult women often receive late diagnoses due to masking. (Source: National Guideline for ASD Assessment Australia)
source_refs: National Guideline for ASD Assessment Australia

options.text: Refer for borderline personality disorder assessment with DBT consideration; defer autism workup pending personality diagnosis
options.rationale: Sensory sensitivity, masking and restricted interests are not core BPD features; misattribution to BPD is a common pitfall in autistic women. (Source: RANZCP: ASD in Adults)
source_refs: RANZCP: Autism Spectrum Disorder in Adults

options.text: Recommend reading on social skills training and self-directed online ASD modules; review at 6 months
options.rationale: Self-directed reading does not substitute for assessment; missing the diagnosis prolongs distress and burnout and delays NDIS access. (Source: RANZCP: ASD in Adults)
source_refs: RANZCP: Autism Spectrum Disorder in Adults

explanation.summary: Autism in adult women is frequently missed because of camouflaging or masking behaviour, internalising symptoms, restricted interests in socially typical topics, and overlapping diagnoses such as anxiety, eating disorders and BPD. Formal assessment uses the Autism Diagnostic Observation Schedule (ADOS-2), Autism Diagnostic Interview-Revised (ADI-R) and developmental history with collateral informants. Diagnosis enables NDIS access, autism-specific supports and reframing of previous diagnoses.
key_points: Adult women with ASD often masked or misdiagnosed
Sensory sensitivity, narrow interests, and burnout cues to assessment
Gold-standard assessment uses ADOS-2 plus developmental history
Diagnosis enables NDIS support and reframing
explanation.pearls: RAADS-R cutoff above 65 and AQ-50 above 32 in adults are suggestive but not diagnostic; formal multidisciplinary assessment is needed.

sources.label: RANZCP: Autism Spectrum Disorder in Adults
National Guideline for ASD Assessment Australia

stem: You are the inpatient psychiatry registrar reviewing a 27-year-old man on admission. Over the past 8 weeks he has been hearing third-person voices commenting on his actions and believing the government is monitoring him through his phone. For the last 3 weeks of that period he has been elated, sleeping 2 hours per night, hyperactive and grandiose, spending $20,000 on cryptocurrency. He has a prior depressive episode 18 months ago. He denies substance use, urine drug screen negative.

data_table.Examination: Pressured speech, flight of ideas, grandiose delusions, third-person auditory hallucinations; thought broadcasting; no insight
data_table.Investigations: FBC, U&E, LFTs, TSH normal; urine drug screen negative; CT head normal

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate first-line treatment?

options.text: Bipolar I disorder with mood-congruent psychosis; start olanzapine 10 mg nocte and lithium 750 mg nocte with monitoring
options.rationale: Psychosis predates the mood episode by 5 weeks, which does not fit mood-congruent psychotic bipolar; schizoaffective should be considered. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

options.text: Schizophreniform disorder; commence risperidone 2 mg twice daily and refer to early psychosis intervention service
options.rationale: Schizophreniform does not account for clearly demarcated mood episode; the presence of a 3-week manic episode within the psychotic illness shifts the formulation. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

options.text: Substance-induced psychosis; observe over 7 days off all substances and reassess for diagnostic clarification
options.rationale: Urine drug screen is negative and the presentation extends over 8 weeks beyond plausible intoxication or withdrawal; substance-induced disorder is unlikely. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

options.text: Schizoaffective disorder bipolar type; commence olanzapine 10 mg nocte plus mood stabiliser (lithium) with ongoing monitoring
options.rationale: Psychotic symptoms persisting at least 2 weeks without mood symptoms within an illness that includes a 3-week manic episode meets schizoaffective bipolar criteria; antipsychotic plus mood stabiliser is first line. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

options.text: Brief psychotic disorder; commence aripiprazole 10 mg daily; review for resolution over 1 month before further treatment
options.rationale: Brief psychotic disorder is limited to less than 1 month; 8 weeks of symptoms and a co-occurring mood episode rule it out. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

explanation.summary: Schizoaffective disorder is diagnosed when psychotic symptoms persist for at least 2 weeks in the absence of prominent mood symptoms, within an illness that also includes a major mood episode (manic for bipolar type, depressive for depressive type) over a substantial portion of the total illness duration. Bipolar I with psychotic features requires psychotic symptoms occurring only during mood episodes. Differentiating the two has prognostic and treatment implications: schizoaffective typically requires both antipsychotic and mood stabiliser long term.
key_points: Schizoaffective: psychosis 2+ weeks without mood symptoms in same illness
Bipolar with psychosis: psychosis only during mood episode
Schizoaffective treatment: antipsychotic plus mood stabiliser
Long-term prognosis intermediate between schizophrenia and bipolar
explanation.pearls: Olanzapine plus lithium is a common Australian first-line combination for schizoaffective bipolar type; clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistance.

stem: You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar reviewing a 34-year-old woman admitted with left-sided weakness for 3 days. The neurologist has ruled out stroke (MRI, CTA and CSF normal) and a video EEG of an episode shows preserved consciousness with normal background. She demonstrates Hoover sign with weakness ablating on contralateral hip extension. She works as a teacher and is not facing legal proceedings or insurance claims. She is distressed by her symptoms and wants to return to work.

data_table.Examination: Left hemiparesis 3/5 globally; positive Hoover sign; normal reflexes and tone; intact sensation to pinprick; tremor entrainment positive
data_table.Investigations: MRI brain and CTA normal; CSF normal; video EEG normal background during episode; FBC, U&E, TFT normal

lead_in: What is the most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate management?

options.text: Malingering; document discrepancy, withdraw analgesia and refer to occupational health for fitness for duty
options.rationale: Malingering requires external incentive (e.g. compensation, legal advantage); none is identified, and the patient is distressed and wishes to return to work, against malingering. (Source: DSM-5-TR; AAN FND Guidance)

options.text: Factitious disorder; transfer to psychiatry under involuntary section for ongoing observation and confrontation with team
options.rationale: Factitious disorder involves intentional symptom production for the sick role; positive rule-in signs of FND such as Hoover sign suggest a positive diagnosis of FND, not factitious. (Source: AAN FND Guidance)

options.text: Somatic symptom disorder; commence sertraline 50 mg daily and refer for CBT for somatic symptoms over 12 weeks
options.rationale: SSD requires excessive thoughts, feelings or behaviours related to somatic symptoms; the central feature here is positive rule-in motor signs, consistent with FND rather than SSD. (Source: DSM-5-TR)

options.text: Stroke mimic with conversion overlay; arrange thrombectomy review and add aspirin 100 mg daily for secondary prevention
options.rationale: Stroke has been excluded by MRI and CTA; secondary prevention is not warranted and labelling stroke risks unnecessary medication. (Source: NHMRC Stroke Guidelines)

options.text: Functional neurological disorder; explain diagnosis using positive signs and refer to physiotherapy with FND-specific approach
options.rationale: FND is diagnosed positively from rule-in signs such as Hoover sign and tremor entrainment, not by exclusion; explaining the diagnosis using the positive signs plus referral to FND-experienced physiotherapy is first-line. (Source: AAN FND Guidance)

explanation.summary: Functional neurological disorder is diagnosed positively using rule-in neurological signs (Hoover sign for limb weakness, tremor entrainment, dissociative seizure features on video EEG with preserved consciousness) rather than by exclusion. Patient explanation using the positive signs themselves and referral to FND-experienced physiotherapy and psychological therapy form the core management. Malingering and factitious disorder require evidence of intentional symptom production with or without external incentives, which is uncommon and not implied by clinical examination findings.
key_points: FND is a positive rule-in diagnosis using neurological signs
Patient explanation grounded in the positive signs is key to engagement
FND-specific physiotherapy and psychological therapy are first line
Malingering needs evidence of external incentive
explanation.pearls: FND has a similar prevalence to multiple sclerosis in neurology clinics; early diagnosis and FND-specific physio improve recovery, especially within the first 6 months.
